coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots incorporated keratin/polyvinyl alcohol hydrogels: Preparation and photoluminescent assessment
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Chohye
Pant, Bishweshwar
Kim, Byoung-Suhk
Jang, Rae-Sang
Park, Seungil
Park, Mira
Park, Soo-Jin
Kim, Hak-Yong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a fabrication of photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) incorporated keratin/poly (vinyl alcohol) (keratin/PVA) hydrogel via an electron beam irradiation technique. Hydrogels with porous structure along with homogeneous networks were obtained by this ecofriendly approach. The prepared hydrogels showed excitation dependant photoluminescent (PL) emission similar to the C-dots. The well preserved quantum confinement effect of the C-dots in the hydrogel can extend its application to biological analysis and medical diagnosis in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Keratin/PVA
Carbon dots
Hydrogel
Photoluminescent

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Commercializations Promotion Agency for R&D Outcomes (COMPA) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (MSIP) (No. 2016K000206). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean Government (MSIP) (No. 2014R1A4A1008140).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of the hydrogels with different formulations.
FT-IR spectra for the prepared samples.
PL spectra of C-dots as compared to the different formulation of hydrogels.
Photographs of the different hydrogels under visible light (A) and UV-light (B). C represents the swelling behavior of hydrogels.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots incorporated keratin/polyvinyl alcohol hydrogels: Preparation and photoluminescent assessment

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of BIN Convergence Technology, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, Republic of Korea (H.-Y. Kim).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a fabrication of photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) incorporated keratin/poly (vinyl alcohol) (keratin/PVA) hydrogel via an electron beam irradiation technique. Hydrogels with porous structure along with homogeneous networks were obtained by this ecofriendly approach. The prepared hydrogels showed excitation dependant photoluminescent (PL) emission similar to the C-dots. The well preserved quantum confinement effect of the C-dots in the hydrogel can extend its application to biological analysis and medical diagnosis in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels are hydrophilic three-dimensional (3D) polymer networks in which the polymer chains are chemically cross-linked or physically connected. Due to the excellent water swelling capacity and bio-compatibility, the hydrogels have been exploited for various applications, particularly in the medical and pharmaceuticals sectors [1]. Generally, natural polymers are preferred in the preparation of hydrogels as they promote tissue growth and differentiation in culture. Like many naturally-derived biomolecules, keratins have intrinsic biological activity and biocompatibility which makes it a suitable candidate for biomedical application. Moreover, extracted keratin can form a self-assembled structures that regulate cellular recognition and behavior [2]. These qualities have led to the development of keratin based nanocomposites as an excellent biomaterial with applications in wound healing, drug delivery, tissue engineering, trauma, and medical devices [3]. In the past, our group has already reported the successful preparation of keratin/poly (vinyl alcohol) hydrogel by electron beam irradiation (EBI) technique for wound dressing applications [4]. The radiation technique is useful for preparing hydrogels, which simultaneously combines cross-linking and sterilization effects producing a toxic free product [5].

In the last decades, photoluminescent carbon-dots (C-dots) have attracted significant attention due to their unique size, less toxicity, good compatibility, and relatively easy surface modification [6–8]. Due to these properties, the C-dots have been applied widely in the field of bioimaging, sensor, electrical devices [9–11]. Recently, we have developed optically transparent and photoluminescent C-dots incorporated polymeric nanofibers by electrospinning process [12]. In this work, we designed a photoluminescent hydrogel utilizing C-dots as a luminescent agent and keratin/PVA hydrogel as a porous materials with a fibrous network which might find suitable application in biomedical field such as bioimaging and drug delivery.
C-dots were synthesized by the microwave assisted method according to our previous report [13]. (Experimental detail can be seen in Supporting information).

First, the keratin was extracted from human hair by sulfitolysis process [12]. Keratin/PVA blend solution (10%) was prepared by adding PVA (MW=85,000–124,000g/mol, 87–89% hydrolyzed) directly into the keratin aqueous solution. The glyoxal solution (10wt%) was then added into the polymeric solution for cross-linking. The keratin/PVA/C-dots blend solutions containing 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1.0wt% of C-dots were prepared by adding the required amount of C-dots by stirring for 6h. The blended solution was poured into the square dishes and irradiated with an electron-beam at a dose of 10kGy (beam energy of 2.5MeV, beam current of 8.5mA, irradiation width of 110cm, conveyor velocity of 10m/min, dose rate of 6.67kGy/s, EBTECH Co., Ltd., Korea) at room temperature. After gelation, the hydrogels were cut into the required sizes (125 mm×125 mm×5 mm) for conducting various analyses.

The interaction between the C-dots, keratin, and PVA in the resulting hydrogel was studied by FTIR. For the pristine C-dots, a broad absorption band at 3100–3500 cm -1 was absorbed which is attributed to OH and NH group [12,14]. The absorption bands at 1617–1770cm−1 are assigned to CO group which indicate the surface carboxylic group. The absorption band at 1570 cm -1 indicated the amide (CONH) bending [12]. In the case of keratin/PVA hydrogel, we obtained similar results as in our previous experiment [12]. A broad peak at 3100–3600 cm−1 was seen which might be attributed to the hydrogen bond between the polymer backbones in NH2 groups of keratin and OH group of PVA. In the keratin/PVA/C-dots hydrogels, it was observed that the addition of C-dots caused a decrease in intensities of the absorption bands at 1661cm−1 and broadening the peak with the increasing concentration of C-dots. The broadening the peak of keratin/PVA in the presence of C-dots might be resulted from the further interaction and the formation of hydrogen bonding between surface functional groups of C-dots and available functional groups of keratin/PVA hydrogel. The chemical bond changes after EBI treatment was investigated by comparing the FTIR spectra of the keratin/PVA/C-dots-1% with and without EBI treatment. (Supporting information Fig. S1). In both cases, we observed similar type of spectra without significant shifting of the characteristic peaks. However, the peak intensities were found to be decreased after EBI treatment which suggests the better interaction between the carbon dots and hydrogels. An extra peak for N1s at 399.13 in the XPS spectrum of C-dots incorporated hydrogels as compared to the keratin/PVA hydrogel (Supporting Information Fig. S2) further confirms the successful incorporation of C-dots in keratin/PVA hydrogel.

C-dots are known for the excitation dependent PL emissions at room temperature [13,15]. When the C-dots solution was excited at the wavelength of 360nm, 480nm, and 545nm, we observed three emission spectra in the wavelength of 483, 532, and 615nm, respectively (Fig. 2 A). The emission spectrum at 483nm was pronounced stronger as compared to other emission spectra at 532 and 615nm. The hydrogels blended with C-dots were also expected to exhibit the same characteristic PL performance. No peaks associated with the C-dots were observed in the case of keratin/PVA hydrogel (Fig. 2B). The keratin/PVA/C-dots hydrogels blended with different amount of C-dots (Fig. 2(C–F) emitted excitation dependent emission spectra at the wavelength of 429nm, 532nm, and 610nm during excitation at the wavelength of 360, 480, and 545nm, respectively. It can be noticed that the C-dots incorporated hydrogels dominantly emitted PL spectra at the wavelength of 429nm whereas the other emission spectra (532nm and 610nm) were less pronounced which is similar to the luminescent property of the C-dots (Fig. 2A). The obtained results showed that the PL spectra of the C-dots incorporated hydrogels were slightly blue shifted as compared to the C-dots. The interaction between the C-dots and keratin/PVA hydrogel may be responsible for the shifting of the PL spectra [13]. As compared to the others, the keratin/PVA/C-dots (0.05%) exhibited strong excitation peaks indicating the highest luminescent property (Fig. 2C). The UV–vis spectra of C-dots free hydrogel did not show any peak in the region 300–800nm, whereas the C-dots incorporated hydrogels showed characteristic peaks at ∼400nm (Supporting Information Fig. S3).

The photographs of the different formulations of hydrogel and their luminescent properties under UV-light irradiation are given in Fig. 3 . As in the Fig. 3A, the keratin/PVA hydrogel is white in color under visible light. The color of the keratin/PVA hydrogel converted to brown after addition of C-dots and the color become much darker with increasing content of C-dots. Under the UV light irradiation, the hydrogels exhibited the fluorescent property and it was strongly pronounced in the case of keratin/PVA/C-dots (0.05%) hydrogel (Fig. 3B). The photographs also confirm the well distribution of C-dots in the hydrogel. Swelling property is an important property of the hydrogel if it is utilized for the biomedical applications. Therefore, we studied the influence of the C-dots on the swelling property of the resulting hydrogels in water (Fig. 3C). The obtained results showed that presence of C-dots suppresses the swelling capacity of the hydrogels. However, the keratin/PVA/C-dot 0.05% hydrogels possess a slightly lower swelling capacity as compared to the C-dots free hydrogel, which suggests that incorporating a small amount of C-dots does not affect the swelling behavior of the hydrogel and it can be applied for the biomedical applications.

C-dots incorporated keratin/PVA hydrogels with porous structure along with homogeneous networks were successfully prepared by a facile route to exploit the photoluminescent property of C-dots and biocompatibility of the keratin/PVA hydrogels. The obtained results may lead to the development of a new family of advanced hydrogel consisting of C-dots and polymer for different applications such as optoelectronic devices, metal ion detection, and biomedical applications. We hope, this method can be extended to fabricate a variety of polymer hydrogels for different applications such as biological analysis and medical diagnosis in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Commercializations Promotion Agency for R&D Outcomes (COMPA) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (MSIP) (No. 2016K000206). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) Grant funded by the Korean Government (MSIP) (No. 2014R1A4A1008140).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.07.058.
